title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Universal Basic Income (UBI)

texts.A: The proposal for a Universal Basic Income is often dismissed as a utopian fantasy by those who have not seriously examined the evidence, yet it is, in fact, a pragmatic and necessary response to the looming automation of the workforce. Millions of jobs in manufacturing, retail, and logistics will disappear within the next two decades, and our current social safety nets are entirely unprepared for this profound disruption. [38]By decoupling survival from traditional employment, we allow individuals the freedom to pursue education, caregiving, or entrepreneurial ventures without the specter of destitution.[/38] Critics argue it would lead to mass idleness, but pilot programs in Finland and Kenya suggest the opposite: [37]people use the floor of financial security to leap into more meaningful and productive work.[/37] It is not a handout; it is a foundational right in a post-industrial economy where the old rules of labor simply no longer apply to everyone.
texts.B: [40]Implementing UBI would be an economic catastrophe of unprecedented proportions.[/40] The sheer cost of providing a meaningful monthly payment to every single citizen would necessitate tax hikes of a scale that would stifle business investment and drive skilled talent abroad to more competitive economies. Furthermore, the profound psychological importance of 'work' cannot be overstated; it provides structure, purpose, social integration, and a fundamental sense of personal dignity that a government cheque simply cannot replace. We evolved as productive beings, and a society without the discipline of purposeful work risks profound cultural stagnation and disengagement. [37]Replacing the dignity of earned income with a state stipend risks creating a permanent underclass characterized by apathy and a fundamental loss of personal agency.[/37] Policy must empower people, not foster dependency on the state. [39]We should focus on targeted wage subsidies and comprehensive retraining programs, not unconditional cash payments.[/39] Efficiency and genuine purpose must guide social policy, not idealism.
texts.C: While I share the reasonable skepticism regarding the fiscal viability of a full-scale UBI, we cannot ignore the fact that our current welfare systems are deeply paternalistic, inefficient, and often counterproductive in their outcomes. A 'negative income tax' or a simplified, universal grant system would eliminate the stifling bureaucratic hurdles that currently trap many working people in a cycle of poverty and institutional dependency. Such a reform would be both cheaper to administer and considerably more dignifying for recipients. However, [39]the idea that UBI alone will solve the automation crisis is naive. Without parallel investments in public housing and healthcare, any cash injection will simply be absorbed by rising rents and spiralling private service costs.[/39] Money in people's pockets does not automatically guarantee a roof over their heads or quality healthcare. The focus must therefore be on 'Universal Basic Services'—housing, healthcare, education—rather than just liquid cash. We need systemic solutions, not simple monetary transfers.
texts.D: The debate over UBI is essentially a debate over the nature of human motivation and our fundamental assumptions about what drives people to contribute meaningfully to society. Those who believe people only work under the immediate threat of starvation or poverty have a very dim and thoroughly evidence-free view of our species. Anthropological research consistently demonstrates that human beings are intrinsically motivated by creativity, connection, and contribution. I agree that [38]UBI provides the essential 'exit option' that allows workers to refuse exploitative conditions, thereby forcing the labor market to improve and offer greater dignity.[/38] Unlike those who fear an inevitable economic meltdown, I believe [40]the increased local spending power generated by a well-designed UBI would trigger a genuine grassroots economic renaissance in neglected communities.[/40] The real challenge is not 'can we afford it,' but 'can we afford the enormous social cost of not doing it' as inequality reaches historically unprecedented and destabilizing breaking points.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the psychological impact of receiving an unconditional income?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes UBI risks creating 'apathy' and a loss of 'dignity.' Expert A argues that it actually empowers people to pursue 'more meaningful work' and entrepreneurial ventures.

questions.question: Which expert shares D's opinion about the effect of UBI on the power dynamics of the labor market?
questions.explanation: Expert D says UBI allows workers to 'refuse exploitative conditions.' Expert A similarly describes it as giving individuals 'freedom' and an alternative to 'traditional employment.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the limitations of UBI as a standalone solution?
questions.explanation: Expert C argues that UBI won't work without other investments (housing, etc.). Expert B also argues for alternatives like 'wage subsidies and retraining,' agreeing that simple cash isn't the silver bullet.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the financial consequences of implementing UBI?
questions.explanation: Expert D believes UBI would trigger an 'economic renaissance.' Expert B, however, predicts an 'economic catastrophe' and 'stifled investment.'
